title: Interactive interpreter

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Interactive interpreter; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapters 2-4 and 8: interpreter, first steps, control flow, and errors; atomic topic: Interactive interpreter
sources.claim: The Python REPL evaluates one interactive statement or expression at a time in persistent session state, making it useful for bounded observation before behavior is preserved in a script and test.

canonicalExplanation.summary: The Python REPL evaluates one interactive statement or expression at a time in persistent session state, making it useful for bounded observation before behavior is preserved in a script and test.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The learner evaluates 7 // 3 and repr of a Unicode string, records the observed values, then moves the verified behavior into a named function and test. Your goal is to test an expression in the REPL and transfer the verified behavior into a script. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: The Python REPL evaluates one interactive statement or expression at a time in persistent session state, making it useful for bounded observation before behavior is preserved in a script and test. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: The display hook shows expression representations differently from print, prior imports and bindings persist, multiline input follows interactive rules, and a successful scratch session is not a reproducible program. The learner must reject this documented misconception: REPL expression output is produced by print and any successful interactive sequence can be rerun as a complete script unchanged.

includes: Use the REPL for bounded exploration and distinguish expression results from printed output.
Test an expression in the REPL and transfer the verified behavior into a script.
excludes: The display hook shows expression representations differently from print, prior imports and bindings persist, multiline input follows interactive rules, and a successful scratch session is not a reproducible program.

outcomes: Test an expression in the REPL and transfer the verified behavior into a script.

examples.title: Interactive interpreter: direct language case
examples.situation: The learner evaluates 7 // 3 and repr of a Unicode string, records the observed values, then moves the verified behavior into a named function and test.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: The learner evaluates 7 // 3 and repr of a Unicode string, records the observed values, then moves the verified behavior into a named function and test.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use the REPL for a bounded observation, distinguish display-hook output from print, then preserve explicit imports, state, inputs, and assertions in saved code.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use the REPL for a bounded observation, distinguish display-hook output from print, then preserve explicit imports, state, inputs, and assertions in saved code.

examples.title: Interactive interpreter: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A snippet fails after restarting because it depended on an earlier import, revealing the missing dependency before the idea enters saved source.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A snippet fails after restarting because it depended on an earlier import, revealing the missing dependency before the idea enters saved source.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: The display hook shows expression representations differently from print, prior imports and bindings persist, multiline input follows interactive rules, and a successful scratch session is not a reproducible program.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: REPL expression output is produced by print and any successful interactive sequence can be rerun as a complete script unchanged.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Test an expression in the REPL and transfer the verified behavior into a script.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: REPL expression output is produced by print and any successful interactive sequence can be rerun as a complete script unchanged.
misconceptions.correction: Use the REPL for a bounded observation, distinguish display-hook output from print, then preserve explicit imports, state, inputs, and assertions in saved code.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: REPL expression output is produced by print and any successful interactive sequence can be rerun as a complete script unchanged.

analogy.example: an experiment notebook that becomes a saved procedure executed by one identified interpreter in a known workspace can introduce Interactive interpreter, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: REPL experiments, scripts, environments, and tracebacks
limitations: import caching, module paths, display hooks, indentation grammar, and traceback chaining are Python-specific

recap.summary: You should now be able to test an expression in the REPL and transfer the verified behavior into a script. Keep this boundary in view: The display hook shows expression representations differently from print, prior imports and bindings persist, multiline input follows interactive rules, and a successful scratch session is not a reproducible program.
